---
title: "5.3.1 Cyber security threats"
subtitle: "Phishing and pharming · P194-P196"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.72em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)

## Phishing

### Legitimate-looking emails

<span class="term">Phishing</span> occurs when a cybercriminal sends out legitimate-looking emails to users.

The emails may contain links or attachments that, when initiated, take the user to a fake website; or they may trick the user into responding with personal data.

Examples of personal data include bank account details and credit/debit card details.

## The User Initiates an Action

### Why deletion helps

The email usually appears to be genuine and may appear to come from a known bank or service provider.

The key point is that the recipient has to initiate some act before the phishing scam can cause any harm.

If suspicious emails are deleted or not opened, then phishing attacks will not cause problems.

## Preventing Phishing

### Awareness and links

Textbook prevention advice includes:

::: {.tight-list}
- users should be aware of new phishing scams
- security awareness training helps identify phishing and pharming scams
- do not click email links unless totally certain it is safe
- fake emails can often be identified by wording such as "Dear Customer"
:::

## Preventing Phishing

### Browser and account checks

The textbook also recommends:

::: {.tight-list}
- run anti-phishing toolbars on browsers
- look out for `https` or the green padlock symbol in the address bar
- check online accounts regularly and maintain passwords
- use an up-to-date browser and a good firewall
- be wary of pop-ups and close them rather than clicking "cancel"
:::

## Spear Phishing

### Specific targets

<span class="term">Spear phishing</span> targets specific individuals or companies to gain access to sensitive financial information or industrial espionage.

Regular phishing is not specific regarding who the victims are.

## Pharming

### Redirected without user action

<span class="term">Pharming</span> is malicious code installed on a user's computer or on an infected website.

The code redirects the user's browser to a fake website without the user's knowledge.

Unlike phishing, the user does not actually need to take any action for it to be initiated.

## Pharming Threat

### Fraud and identity theft

The creator of the malicious code can gain personal data, such as bank details, from the user.

Often the website appears to come from a trusted source and can lead to <span class="mark">fraud and identity theft</span>.

## DNS Cache Poisoning

### Changing IP address values

Redirection from a legitimate website to a fake website can be done using <span class="term">DNS cache poisoning</span>.

Every time a user types in a URL, the browser contacts the DNS server and the IP address is sent back. DNS cache poisoning changes the real IP address values to those of the fake website.

## Reducing Pharming Risk

### Checks and browser warnings

It is possible to mitigate against pharming:

::: {.tight-list}
- anti-virus software can detect unauthorised alterations to a website address
- modern browsers can alert users to pharming and phishing attacks
- check the spelling of websites to ensure the address is correct
- use `https` or the green padlock symbol as an additional defence
:::

If the DNS server itself has been infected, rather than the user's computer, it is much more difficult to mitigate the risk.

## Activity 5.3

### Textbook practice

The textbook asks students to:

::: {.tight-list}
- describe data security issues for a company communicating over the internet
- explain why each issue threatens company security
- describe ways to mitigate those threats
- explain worm, ransomware and Trojan horse
- judge weak passwords and improve password maintenance
:::

## Classroom Check

### Phishing and pharming

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- phishing uses legitimate-looking emails with links or attachments
- phishing needs the recipient to initiate an action
- spear phishing targets specific people or companies
- pharming redirects a browser to a fake website without the user's knowledge
- DNS cache poisoning changes real IP address values to fake website addresses
- anti-virus software, modern browser alerts, correct spelling and https/padlock checks help reduce risk
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Social engineering

The next deck explains how cybercriminals manipulate people rather than break systems directly.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)
